retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of ECC depends on the stage at which it is found, the involvement of the parents, and the capacity to maintain appropriate hygiene and dietary habits in the long term. If the disease is diagnosed in the early stages, the caries process can be stopped in time through preventive measures and education, reversing the carious lesions, and protecting the unaffected teeth. By contrast, severe cases of ECC that remain untreated have a poor prognosis: impaired nutrition, chronic anemia, and growth retardation, affecting the child's quality of life and general health.

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dental caries is the most prevalent chronic disease worldwide. [1] It is a biofilm-mediated, diet-modulated, multifactorial, and non-transmissible disease. Its dynamic pathogenesis involving mineral loss of dental hard tissues results in demineralization and remineralization phases. Biological, behavioral, psychosocial, and environmental factors are also associated with the development of the condition. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Function -- Pathophysiology of Dental Caries
retrieved_docs.content: All the tooth surfaces are susceptible to caries throughout an individual's lifetime. But, the disease will not occur unless teeth are exposed to cariogenic biofilm and frequent sugar consumption. The modern concept of caries also considers other factors, such as sociodemographic situation, access to dental care, and fluoride exposure, which are significant but not decisive to develop the disease. For all of this, dental caries should be described as a complex biofilm-mediated affection linked mostly to frequent ingestion of sugars and poor oral hygiene combined with inadequate fluoride exposure. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: At first, the maxillary incisors present a white demineralization band along the gingival margin, usually missed by parents. The demineralization bands gradually progress to cavities that encircle the teeth' necks in a brown-black collar. By contrast, the lower incisors are unaffected - either completely healthy or mildly carious. Severe cases may show the crowns of the upper incisors destroyed, leaving root stumps.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the etiology of dental caries. Summarize the pathophysiology of dental caries, highlighting the currently accepted theory. Describe dental caries' clinical findings. Explain the treatment considerations for patients with dental caries. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Often, these children come from low-income families who lack access to healthcare, and the condition worsens. With time caries can progress to pulpitis and severe dental abscesses impairing erythropoiesis by affecting inflammation-induced metabolic pathways, causing chronic anemia and growth retardation. [33] [38] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Behavioral, psychological, and social factors also play a significant role in the disease process. [4] Fluoride's capacity to prevent caries is a well-known fact, and insufficient fluoride exposure should also be considered a contributing factor in the disease process. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dentistry dates back to 5000 B.C. when the thinking was that the cause of dental caries was a “tooth worm.” The term “dental caries” was first reported in the literature around 1634, and it originates from the Latin word “caries,” which stands for decay. The term was initially used to describe holes in the teeth. Dental caries is reported to be one of the oldest and most common diseases found in humans. Dental caries is a prevalent chronic infectious disease resulting from tooth-adherent cariogenic bacteria that metabolize sugars to produce acid, which, over time, demineralizes tooth structure.

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Children with ECC may experience significant pain, which makes eating and sometimes talking difficult, and a physical developmental delay will be seen due to poor nutrition. A study showed that children with early childhood caries were significantly shorter and lighter than controls without caries. [33] Because of the altered appearance of their teeth, children with ECC can become socially and psychologically affected, leading to decreased school performance. [34] [35] [36] [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of dental caries in the primary dentition of young children is known as early childhood caries (ECC), which is defined as one or more decayed, missing (resulting from caries) or filled teeth in primary dentition in children of up to 71 months of age. The term early childhood caries include the sometimes called nursing caries and rampant caries. This activity reviews the cause, pathophysiology, and presentation of early childhood caries and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its management.

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Function -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: High risk: patients present one or more risk factors like frequent consumption of dietary carbohydrates (more than four times of sugar per day), inadequate exposure to fluoride, poor oral hygiene, and salivary dysfunction. Although, the best indicator that patients will develop future caries is their past caries history. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- History and Physical -- Recurrent Caries
retrieved_docs.content: A recurrent or secondary caries is a new caries that form at the restoration’s margin. [17] A microleakage of the restoration causes it; however, this leakage does not result in an active demineralization under the filling. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The term early childhood caries includes the sometimes called nursing caries and rampant caries. “Baby bottle syndrome,” “nursing caries,” or “bottle mouth caries” are names to describe a pattern of caries where the deciduous upper incisors and molars are more severely disrupted. A key feature of this type of caries is the sparing of the lower incisors, which can be completely healthy or mildly affected. The term rampant caries is usually given to extensive caries affecting children of three to four years of age that do not follow the nursing caries pattern. Despite early childhood caries can be easily preventable, it is one of the most prevalent childhood diseases worldwide, mainly affecting socially disadvantaged populations. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Treatment / Management -- Newer Treatment Approach
retrieved_docs.content: The traditional restorative and surgical way of managing early childhood caries requires general anesthesia with its attendant health risks and is expensive and does not prevent further caries but only treats the result of the disease. Therefore, up to 79% of patients treated with conventional therapy relapse. [32] This is why the new management of ECC focuses on preventive measures, including parental involvement, preventive programs with active monitoring of the condition, temporary restorations, and postpones advanced restorations.

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: All types of caries, including nursing caries, result from the interaction of cariogenic microbiota, fermentable carbohydrates, and susceptible teeth that interact during a prolonged period. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of ECC is complex and is determined by many factors, including the age of the child, the extent of dental damage, and concomitant complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2779
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with both anxiety (القلق) and oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). Anxiety can manifest as vague chest discomfort, making it an important psychosomatic contributor to this symptom. Oral candidiasis, a fungal infection of the mouth, may also be linked to nonspecific chest pain, potentially through systemic effects, medication side‑effects, or underlying immunosuppression that predisposes patients to both conditions. Recognizing that nonspecific chest pain may signal either anxiety or oral thrush—or a combination of both—helps clinicians pursue a more comprehensive evaluation and tailor management to address the underlying cause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that an uncontrolled disease state is linked to the presence of diabetes mellitus that has progressed to a complicated form, underscoring the importance of effective disease management to prevent or mitigate diabetic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1726
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety (القلق) is characterized by fatigue (التَّعَب) and may present with nonspecific chest pain. Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is also clinically associated with both nonspecific chest pain and fatigue. The overlap of fatigue and nonspecific chest pain in anxiety and oral candidiasis highlights the need for careful clinical assessment to differentiate between psychological and infectious etiologies, ensuring appropriate diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 5 -- Community 2933
retrieved_docs.content: Fatigue (التَّعَب) is described as having anxiety (القلق) as a defining characteristic, indicating that patients who experience fatigue often also report significant anxious feelings. Clinically, fatigue is also associated with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي), suggesting that the presence of persistent tiredness may coincide with or signal an underlying yeast infection of the mouth. Recognizing the link between fatigue, anxiety, and oral candidiasis can aid clinicians in evaluating patients holistically, prompting assessment for both psychological distress and possible infectious contributors when fatigue is reported.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1923
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي is a medical condition that is associated with ذَبْهَةُ برنزميتال. Clinically, التصلب العصيدي is linked to several findings, including النفخة الانقباضية, حفظ الصحة الروتيني, عرج, and لغط الشريان السباتي. In addition, التصلب العصيدي has a recognized subtype called التصلب العصيدي المعمم, reflecting a more generalized form of the disease. These associations are important for recognizing the clinical presentation of التصلب العصيدي and guiding appropriate evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier “rehabilitace” is applicable as a therapeutic approach for the conditions قرحة قلاعية, الالتصاق, and اِضطِراب اللسان, indicating that rehabilitation can be employed in the management of these oral health disorders.

Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2614
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is described as a general clinical state that is interpreted by clinicians (is_interpreted_by) and is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications. This indicates that an uncontrolled disease condition determination reflects a concerning overall clinical status and frequently co‑occurs with complicated diabetes mellitus, highlighting the need for careful assessment and targeted management in patients presenting with such uncontrolled conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1035
retrieved_docs.content: Premature birth, tissue rupture and diabetic nephropathy are each described with the qualifiers chemically‑induced, dietary therapy, epidemiology, ethnology and pharmacotherapy, illustrating that metabolic, developmental and vascular pathologies can arise from chemical exposures and be managed through nutrition, population‑based insights and drug treatment. Anxiety (القلق) is similarly qualified by chemical induction, diet‑based interventions and pharmacotherapy; fatigue (التَّعَب) is identified as a defining feature of anxiety and is clinically linked to nonspecific chest pain, a somatic manifestation that may coexist with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). Chemically‑induced mechanisms are applicable to a broad spectrum of disorders, including gastric ulcer (قَرْهَة مَعِدِيّة), urinary stone (حَصاة), nonspecific chest pain, depression (اكتئاب), mortality (التموت), extra‑hepatic biliary obstruction (انسداد صفراوي خارج الكبد), enteric viral infection (عدوى بفيروس معوي),

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.
